request.question: Which was founded earlier, The Week or New Scientist?

title: Massachusetts Audubon Society
Pirate Party (Italy)
Jordan River Foundation
Champ de Mars Racecourse
Canons Regular of the Holy Sepulchre
The Week
Nominative determinism
Gloucester Old Bank
New Scientist
1883 Philadelphia Quakers season
sentences: The Massachusetts Audubon Society, founded in 1896 by Harriet Hemenway and headquartered in Lincoln, Massachusetts, is a nonprofit organization dedicated to "protecting the nature of Massachusetts."
Mass Audubon is independent of the National Audubon Society, and in fact was founded earlier.
Mass Audubon protects 36,500 acres of land throughout Massachusetts, saving birds and other wildlife, and making nature accessible to all with its wildlife sanctuaries and 20 nature centers.
The Italian Pirate Party (Italian: "Partito Pirata Italiano" ) is a political party in Italy, founded on 16 September 2006, modelled on the Pirate Party of Sweden, founded earlier that year.
It supports reform of copyright and patent law, privacy and freedom of expression.

The "Jordan River Foundation" was founded earlier by Queen Nour Al Hussein, the wife of the late king of Jordan Hussein bin Talal, after the death of king Hussein, queen Nour left her position as the Chair person of "Jordan River Foundation" and the new queen Rania took over the position.
The Champ de Mars Racecourse is a thoroughbred horse race track in Port Louis, Mauritius.

The Racecourse was inaugurated on 25 June 1812, by The Mauritius Turf Club (MTC) which was founded earlier in the same year by Sir Robert Townsend Farquhar, who was the first British Governor of Mauritius.
The Mauritius Turf Club is the oldest horse-racing club in the Southern Hemisphere and the second oldest in the world.
The race track follows a very selective right hand oval path and is relatively small in size, with a circumference of 1,298 meters (4,258.5 ft) and width between 12 and 14 meters (39 and 46 feet).
The home-straight extends uphill and is 225 meters (738 ft) long.

The Canons Regular of the Holy Sepulchre were a Catholic religious order of canons regular of the Rule of Saint Augustine said to have been founded in the Church of the Holy Sepulchre in Jerusalem, then the capital of the Kingdom of Jerusalem, recognised in 1113 by Papal bull of Pope Paschal II.
Other accounts has it that they were founded earlier, during the rule of Godfrey of Bouillon (1099–1100).
The Week is a weekly British news magazine founded in 1995 which has also published a US edition since 2001 and a children's edition in the UK since 2015.
Between 2008 and 2012 it also published an Australian edition.
Nominative determinism is the hypothesis that people tend to gravitate towards areas of work that fit their names.
The term was first used in the magazine "New Scientist" in 1994, after the magazine's humorous Feedback column noted several studies carried out by researchers with remarkably fitting surnames.
These included a book on polar explorations by Daniel Snowman and an article on urology by researchers named Splatt and Weedon.
These and other examples led to light-hearted speculation that some sort of psychological effect was at work.
Since the term appeared, nominative determinism has been an irregularly recurring topic in "New Scientist", as readers continue to submit examples.
Nominative determinism differs from the related concept aptronym, and its synonyms aptonym, namephreak, and Perfect Fit Last Name, in that it focusses on causality.
"Aptronym" merely means the name is fitting, without saying anything about why it has come to fit.
The Gloucester Old Bank was a British bank that operated between 1716 and 1838.
It was founded in 1716 by James Wood.
The bank was said to have been the oldest private bank in Britain, having survived the financial consequences of the Napoleonic Wars when many other banks went out of business.
The claim was wrong as both C. Hoare & Co and Child & Co. were founded earlier, it was, however, one of the oldest banks in Britain in the nineteenth century.
New Scientist is a weekly English-language international science magazine, founded in 1956.
Since 1996 it has also run a website.
The 1883 Philadelphia Quakers season was the first in the franchise's existence.
The team was founded earlier in the year as a replacement for the Worcester franchise.
It was the first year Philadelphia was represented in the National League since the original Athletics were disbanded in 1876.
The American Association's Philadelphia Athletics had been founded a year earlier.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

You are the first-hop summarizer. Compress the supplied passages into a short, actionable intermediate state that lets the next agent know exactly what is present in the passages, what is missing, and what to fetch next. Produce a single `summary` string only.

Summary content requirements (concise: a few short sentences or clauses):
- First-hop evidence: one short paraphrase or a very brief quoted phrase from the passages that links the relevant entities or facts supporting any partial answer. Do not copy long verbatim spans.
- Bridge entity: explicitly name the person/entity mentioned in the passages that the second hop should investigate (use the full name or label exactly as it appears in the passages). If the question uses only a given name and the passages do not disambiguate which individual is meant, state clearly that the identity is missing/ambiguous.
- Missing fact: state in one short sentence which specific fact is required to answer the question (for example: winner name, year, birthplace, or another identifying detail) and confirm it is not present in the given passages.
- Retrieval directions: give one concise, ordered, actionable next-hop plan (1–2 steps). Prefer concrete target pages or query patterns, e.g. "open the named person's biography to check for [missing fact]" or a short query pattern such as '"<NAME>" birthplace OR "born in" site:wikipedia.org' or 'open a chronological list of <AWARD> winners and search for <NAME>'. Do not instruct the system to execute retrieval here — only specify what the next retrieval should fetch.
- Clarification: if ambiguity persists (e.g., only a given name is provided and multiple candidates are possible), include a short clarifying request asking the user to provide the full name.

Hard constraints and tone:
- Be concise and factual. Keep the same neutral, summary tone as before.
- Do not guess, infer, or assert any facts not present in the passages (no candidate answers, no years, no birthplace assertions, etc.).
- Do not ask the user for permission to search external sources. Give explicit next-hop retrieval directions instead.
- Output only the single `summary` field text complying with the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict: Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

You are integrating newly retrieved passages (second‑hop evidence) with the first‑hop context to produce a grounded, post‑retrieval summary that preserves the facts the final answer generator will need. Produce a single field `summary` whose value is 1–3 concise sentences that: (1) directly state the answer to the question (e.g., where the studios/recordings were located) based on the available evidence; (2) if any passage explicitly names a production facility or studio and ties that facility to a specific locality (for example by giving the facility name together with a street, town, or county), include one short supporting sentence that paraphrases that passage to show the link between the facility and the locality; and (3) include one very short sentence or clause that synthesizes the context and the supporting passage to place the studios in the stated locality. Paraphrase supporting passages; do not include long verbatim quotations.

If the passages do not conclusively link a named studio to a specific locality, preserve and state that uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported specifics. Avoid headers, meta commentary, long quotations, extra sections, or inventing details not present in the provided context/passages. Keep phrasing compact, factual, and focused on the evidence needed to support the final answer.

Output contract: produce only the `summary` field (1–3 concise sentences) that integrates the first‑hop context with the second‑hop passages as described above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer` as follows. Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) as the evidential basis for the final answer; do not add external facts except where the instructions below explicitly allow a limited, well‑justified use of internal background knowledge. Always produce the final result inside the [[ ## answer ## ]] block and make that block contain only the minimal answer string required by these rules.

1) General minimal-answer rule
- If the summaries unambiguously support a single short answer (a short name, title, demonym, year, or single noun/phrase of roughly 1–5 words), emit only that minimal token sequence as the answer. Normalize the chosen substring by removing any leading/trailing whitespace or surrounding punctuation but preserve the phrase's internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries. Do not add quotes, em dashes, braces, labels, trailing punctuation, explanatory clauses, or any additional text.

2) When both summaries agree on an identical short phrase
- If summary_1 and summary_2 contain the same short named-entity string (after lowercasing only for agreement detection), extract that common phrase, strip surrounding punctuation/whitespace, and emit it verbatim (preserving original capitalization) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]] with no surrounding or appended characters.

3) Patterned question triggers (minimal formatting + deterministic selection)
- 'Which ... is from X, A or B?': When the question asks which of two named options is from a place and the summaries clearly indicate a single band/person as the answer, emit only the minimal band/person name supported by the summaries.
- Comparative membership questions (e.g., 'Does A or B have more band members?'): If the summaries clearly indicate which band has more members, emit only that band's name.
- Short common-name questions (e.g., 'What do people in the UK commonly call the location ...?'): If both summaries provide the same short common name, emit only that short name string.

4) Nationality questions
- If the question asks for a nationality/demonym and either summary_1 or summary_2 contains a demonym, emit only the minimal demonym token/phrase. Prefer the first demonym found in summary_1; if none appears in summary_1, use the first found in summary_2. Emit it verbatim (no added words or punctuation).

5) Two-performer composition specificity
- If the question asks for the "type of musical composition" used by a named act and both summaries use a generic word like 'song(s)', you may consult internal knowledge only to determine whether the act is a two-person (duo) group. If you can reliably confirm the act is a two-person group, replace the generic term with the single-word or very short noun that names a composition intended for two performers (for example, 'duet') and emit that single word as the minimal answer. If you cannot reliably confirm duo membership, emit the minimal noun exactly as it appears in the summaries.

6) Narrow assumed referent (single-name trigger)
- If the question supplies only a given name (e.g., 'Maurice') and the visible summaries explicitly mention a specific example with that given name (e.g., a named example appears in the summaries) and the summaries identify the requested fact (e.g., the award), then you may assume that referent and emit the minimal answer (for example, a single year) if you have sufficient internal confidence. If you do not have sufficient internal confidence, follow the normal clarification behavior (emit a brief clarifying question as the answer field) rather than guessing.

7) What to avoid (formatting and content prohibitions)
- Never add explanatory text, full sentences, comparisons, justifications, or provenance in the answer field. Do not append decorative punctuation (em dashes, extra commas, trailing periods), quotes, braces, JSON-like structures, leading newlines, or any characters not part of the normalized minimal phrase. Do not invent facts not present in the summaries except where the two narrowly scoped rules above permit limited internal confirmation (duo-confirmation and the single-name assumed referent). If the summaries do not unambiguously support a single minimal answer and you lack permitted internal justification, emit a concise clarification request as the answer.

Additional specific, evidence-directed selection rules (apply these only when their trigger conditions are met, otherwise follow the rules above):

8) Prefer an explicitly named immediate jurisdiction when resolving county/country wording conflicts
- Trigger: The question asks which country/sovereign-state a county or borough is part of (phrasing like 'county' and 'part of what country' or equivalent) and the summaries disagree or one summary uses a parenthetical membership note such as 'A (part of B)'.
- Behavior: If one summary contains a parenthetical of the form 'A (part of B)', treat the pre-parenthetical name A as the immediate administrative region referenced by the summaries and emit A (trimmed of surrounding punctuation/whitespace) as the minimal answer. More generally, when summaries disagree about a place, prefer the summary that directly names the immediate jurisdiction associated with the county (the subnational or constituent area named in the summary) and extract that short named phrase verbatim as the answer. Do not add any extra words or punctuation.
- Fallback: If neither summary clearly names an immediate jurisdiction or both are equally ambiguous, emit a concise clarification question per the base rules rather than guessing.

9) Size-answer simplification when both summaries agree with an inclusive qualifier
- Trigger: The question explicitly asks for a size (phrasing like 'what size' or 'equal to or smaller than what size' or equivalent) AND summary_1 and summary_2 are identical strings for agreement-detection (case-insensitive) AND that common string matches the pattern '<size> or smaller' or '<size> or less' (case-insensitive, allowing optional surrounding punctuation/whitespace).
- Behavior: Produce as the minimal answer only the '<size>' portion of the agreed common phrase by removing the trailing inclusive qualifier 'or smaller' or 'or less' and any immediately adjacent punctuation/extra whitespace. Emit the remaining size token (number plus unit) verbatim as shown in the summaries (preserve digits, unit spelling/capitalization, and internal spacing).
- Do not apply this edit when the summaries do not exactly agree, when the common phrase does not match the pattern, or when the question is not asking for a size.

10) Prefer identical parenthetical short-names when present in both summaries
- Trigger: Both summary_1 and summary_2 each contain an identical parenthetical short-name substring (a substring of the form '(' ... ')' that, once trimmed, is 1–5 words long, agreement checked case-insensitively).
- Behavior: Extract the parenthetical content (remove the surrounding '(' and ')', trim surrounding whitespace and surrounding punctuation) and treat that extracted short-name as the canonical minimal answer. Emit it verbatim (preserving internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]]. Do not emit the longer formal name when such an identical parenthetical is present in both summaries.

Output contract reminder: The produced `answer` must be exactly the minimal answer string determined by the rules above (or a short clarification question when required). The [[ ## answer ## ]] block must contain only that string and nothing else.

response.hop1_query: Which was founded earlier, The Week or New Scientist?
hop1_docs: New Scientist | New Scientist is a weekly English-language international science magazine, founded in 1956.  Since 1996 it has also run a website.

The New Times (magazine) | The New Times (Russian: Но́вые Времена́ ) is a Russian language magazine in Russia.  The magazine was founded in 1943.  The current version, established in 1988, , is a liberal, independent Russian weekly news magazine, publishing for Russia and Armenia.  (During the Soviet times it was a multi-language political magazine which followed the official party line.)  Its chief editor is Russian investigative journalist, political scientist, writer and radio host Yevgenia Albats.

Bat Boy (character) | Bat Boy is a creature who made several appearances in the American supermarket tabloid "Weekly World News".  "The Weekly World News" published patently fabricated stories which were purported to be factual.  Within the pages of the paper, Bat Boy is described as a creature who is 'half human and half bat'.  His pursuers, according to "Weekly World News", are scientists and United States government officials; he is frequently captured, then later makes a daring escape.  The original scientist who found him was named Dr. Ron Dillon.  Matthew Daemon, S.O.S. (Seeker of Obscure Supernaturals) crossed paths with him on several occasions.

Vinod Agarwal | Dr. Vinod K. Agarwal is an Indian-American businessman and scientist.  He was a professor at McGill University in Montreal, Canada from 1978 to 1992.  He then founded LogicVision, which he took public in October, 2001, as the first technology IPO following the terrorist attacks earlier that year.  Agarwal left LogicVision in 2005 to start SemIndia, a semiconductor company based in India.

Odisha Sampark Kranti Express | Odisha Sampark Kranti Express is a bi-weekly train which runs between Bhubaneswar,Capital of Odisha and Anand Vihar Terminal.  This train was introduced as part of the Sampark Kranti trains that were announced by the then railway minister Nitish Kumar in 2005.  It was and still is a bi-weekly train.  Now there is a requirement of attaching AC First class berths to this train.  Earlier the train used to terminate at New Delhi railway station (NDLS) but now terminates at Anand Vihar Terminus (ANVT) of New Delhi.  Its rakes are maintained by East Coast Railway (ECoR).

The Aquarian Weekly | The Aquarian Weekly is a regional alternative weekly newspaper based in New Jersey.  Founded in 1969, its focus is rock music and related events in the New Jersey/New York City/Eastern Pennsylvania region.  From 1986 to 1992, it was called East Coast Rocker.  After returning to its original title, the newspaper began including a pull-out section that retained the "East Coast Rocker" name, and which is now freely distributed throughout the region.

New Adventures | New Adventures is a British dance company, specialising in contemporary dance.  Founded by the choreographer Matthew Bourne in 2002, the company developed from an earlier company Adventures in Motion Pictures, which was later dissolved.
response.summary_1: First-hop evidence: passage says "New Scientist ... founded in 1956." Bridge entity: investigate "The Week" (the passages do not mention "The Week" — identity/founding info is missing). Missing fact: the founding year of The Week is required and is not present in the given passages. Retrieval directions: 1) open the "The Week (magazine)" Wikipedia page or the magazine's official history page to find its founding/first-published year; 2) compare that year to New Scientist's 1956. Clarification: if you mean a specific edition (e.g., The Week UK vs The Week US), please specify which one.
response.hop2_query:
What year was The Week magazine first published? Provide the founding/first-publication year for:
- The Week (UK) — include source (Wikipedia or The Week official site).
- The Week (US) — include source (Wikipedia or The Week official site).
If there are other notable national editions with different founding years, list those as well with sources.
hop2_docs: The Week | The Week is a weekly British news magazine founded in 1995 which has also published a US edition since 2001 and a children's edition in the UK since 2015.  Between 2008 and 2012 it also published an Australian edition.

Hit Music | Hit Music was a weekly British chart newsletter; sister publication to "Music Week".  "Hit Music" existed for almost nine years, supplying the official UK music charts (as compiled by Gallup and later OCC/CIN).  The founding editors were Graham Walker and Tony Brown.  The first issue was published 5.09.1992 (chart date: 12.09.1992), the last issue 5.05.2001 (no. 439).

Russian Art Week | Russian Art Week is a major international art fair in London, UK, which takes places bi-annually every June and November.  During the week the UK's four major auction houses Christie's, Sotheby's, MacDougall's and Bonhams present a programme of sales featuring Russian paintings and works of art.  Russian Art Week works in parallel with the salesrooms to provide a comprehensive listing of the season's sales and events.  The website and the related published guide provide a single source for information on the Russian auctions as well as other related cultural events, exhibitions and conferences taking place around London.  It is accompanied by a directory of Russian auction houses, galleries, dealers and specialist institutions.

The Himalayan Beacon | The Himalayan Beacon, known until 2006 simply as "BEACON", is a monthly news-magazine published in Darjeeling by The Mandalay Books India (Pvt.) Ltd., and distributed throughout Darjeeling Hills and Sikkim.  It is now owned by The Mandalay Books India (Pvt.) Ltd., which publishes other periodicals, including the weekly newspaper "This Week" and "The Himalayan Traveller".  "BEACON" is the largest and the first magazine to be published by and for the Darjeeling Hills.  Nicknamed the "Fire Hose" for its colourful appearance and undiplomatic journalistic style, it is often regarded as a source of record, meaning that it is frequently relied upon as the official and authoritative reference for modern events.  Founded in 1998 by Barun Roy, the magazine is by far more popular among the youths of the region than the readers of other generations.  The magazine's name is often abbreviated to "Beacon", due to its earlier popularity as "Beacon".  Its famous motto, always printed in the upper left-hand corner of the cover page, is "News and Views on All for All."

Weekly Markets in Munich | The weekly markets in Munich, Germany, are an important source of food to the residents of the city.  The population of the city is provided with groceries by 41 weekly markets, including farmers’ markets and organic-food markets on a daily basis.  These markets can be found all over Munich.

Hellomagazine.com | Hellomagazine.com is the official website of the weekly celebrity news magazine Hello! , and the UK’s leading celebrity news site.  Started in 2001 to complement the magazine, the site is updated throughout the day, seven days a week.

Billboard Year-End | "Billboard" Year-End charts are a cumulative measure of a single or album's performance in the United States, based upon the "Billboard" magazine charts during any given chart year.  "Billboard's" "chart year" runs from the first Billboard "week" of December to the final week in November, but because the Billboard week is dated in advance of publication, the last calendar week for which sales are counted is usually the third week in November.  This altered calendar allows for "Billboard" to calculate year-end charts and release them in time for its final print issue in the last week of December.  Prior to incorporating chart data from Nielsen SoundScan (from 1991), year-end charts were calculated by an inverse-point system based solely on a title's performance (for example a single appearing on the "Billboard" Hot 100 would be given one point for a week spent at position 100, two points for a week spent at position ninety-nine, and so forth, up to 100 points for each week spent at number one).  Other factors including the total weeks a song spent on the chart and at its peak position were calculated into its year-end total.  The same method was used for albums based on the "Billboard" 200, and songs appearing on the other charts (e.g. Hot Country Singles).
response.summary_2: New Scientist was founded in 1956, so it was founded earlier than The Week. The Week is a British weekly news magazine founded in 1995 (with a US edition launched in 2001), so New Scientist (1956) predates the British magazine The Week (1995).
